perturbation.original_text: Donated amounts will be allocated and dispersed to the Northern California Chapter beginning on the first full quarter [three (3) month period] of the Agreement and continue on a quarterly basis thereafter for the term of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Donated amounts will be allocated and dispersed to the Northern California Chapter beginning 180 days after the end of each calendar quarter and continue on that semi-annual basis thereafter for the term of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment schedule to a semi-annual payment 180 days after the end of the calendar quarter creates a delay in payment that could violate California labor laws regarding timely payments.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 204 requires that wages earned during a calendar month must be paid within specific timeframes. Delaying payments by 180 days after each quarter could be interpreted as violating this requirement, as it significantly extends the permissible timeframe for wage disbursement.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 204. (a) All wages, other than those mentioned in Section 201, 201.3, 202, 204.1, or 204.2, earned by any person in any employment are due and payable twice during each calendar month, on days designated in advance by the employer as the regular paydays. Labor performed between the 1st and 15th days, inclusive, of any calendar month shall be paid for between the 16th and the 26th day of the month during which the labor was performed, and labor performed between the 16th and the last day, inclusive, of any calendar month, shall be paid for between the 1st and 10th day of the following month. However, salaries of executive, administrative, and professional employees of employers covered by the Fair Labor Standards Act, as set forth pursuant to Section 13(a)(1) of the Fair Labor Standards Act, as amended through March 1, 1969, in Part 541 of Title 29 of the Code of Federal Regulations, as that pa
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the payment schedule from quarterly to semi-annual payments 180 days after the end of each calendar quarter. This contradicts California Labor Code Section 204, which requires wages to be paid twice during each calendar month. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "All wages...are due and payable twice during each calendar month...Labor performed between the 1st and 15th days...shall be paid for between the 16th and the 26th day of the month...and labor performed between the 16th and the last day...shall be paid for between the 1st and 10th day of the following month." The changed text's semi-annual payment schedule and 180-day delay clearly violate this requirement for more frequent and timely wage disbursement.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will provide to the NFLA-NC upon request the most recent quarterly sales report of the Company's Licensed Products.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will provide the NFLA-NC access to an annual summary sales report of the Company's Licensed Products at the Company's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the reporting frequency from quarterly to annual, and making it discretionary, reduces transparency and could be seen as violating good faith contractual obligations, especially if it impacts royalty or payment calculations. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, especially under California Law.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making sales reports discretionary and only annual, the modification potentially violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing required in California Commercial Code § 1201 because it creates a situation where the Company could manipulate the reporting to the detriment of NFLA-NC, hindering their ability to verify proper payments.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in possessio
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the reporting frequency from quarterly to annual and makes it discretionary, creating an inconsistency that contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'agreement' broadly, encompassing the 'bargain of the parties' and incorporating 'course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade.' By changing the reporting terms significantly and making them discretionary, the company could manipulate reporting to NFLA-NC's detriment, hindering their ability to verify payments properly. This is a direct contradiction of the implied duty of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: Parties agree to the addition of Gridiron CBD H2O Probiotic™ Water to "Licensed Products" as follows: SECTION ONE. DEFINITIONS As used in this Agreement, the following terms shall be defined as follows: F. "Licensed Products" shall mean BlackMP Living Water, BlackMP Concentrate, Zezel Probiotic Water, Zayin Sports Water, Gridiron CBD H2O Probiotics™ Water, Gridiron MVP™ and Gridiron MVP™ Concentrate using the Pro Football Legends Logo on the Licensed Products' affixed labels, hang-tags or packaging.
perturbation.changed_text: Parties agree to the addition of Gridiron CBD H2O Probiotic™ Water to "Licensed Products." SECTION ONE. DEFINITIONS As used in this Agreement, the following terms shall be defined as follows: F. "Licensed Products" shall mean BlackMP Living Water, BlackMP Concentrate, Zezel Probiotic Water, Zayin Sports Water, Gridiron MVP™ and Gridiron MVP™ Concentrate using the Pro Football Legends Logo on the Licensed Products' affixed labels, hang-tags or packaging. New products may be added to the list of Licensed Products without written consent from all parties.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the need for written consent to add new products could violate contract law principles requiring mutual agreement for material changes to a contract, potentially leading to disputes and unenforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1549 defines a contract as an agreement to do or not to do a certain thing. By allowing the Company to add new products without written consent, the modification undermines the principle of mutual agreement, potentially violating the requirement for a valid and enforceable contract under California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1. NATURE OF A CONTRACT [1549 - 1615] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. Definition [1549 - 1550.5] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) 1549. A contract is an agreement to do or not to do a certain thing. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a legal contradiction by allowing the addition of new products to "Licensed Products" without written consent from all parties. This undermines the principle of mutual agreement required for contract modifications, as stated in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. California Civil Code § 1549 defines a contract as an agreement to do or not to do a certain thing. Allowing unilateral changes to the 'Licensed Products' list contradicts this definition, thus creating a contradiction.
